content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1 R Street
mento 95814
322 5650

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

December 16, 1975

Ward Cameron, Supervisor
District 5
Butte County
County Administration Building
Oroville, Calif. 95965

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-213

Dear Mr. Cameron:

Thank you for your letter of December 4, 1975, requesting an opinion relative to whether you have a conflict of interest which would preclude you from voting on the issue of establishing a special task force. Since your opinion request does not raise a novel question of interpretation under the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be issued.

According to the facts you outline in your letter, the potential conflict of interest about which you are concerned involves a business transaction you had with Mr. Rupp. You state that the transaction was concluded on April 23, 1973, and that you have not done any work for Mr. Rupp since that time. You also state that you do not know whether you will do more work for Mr. Rupp in the future. These facts do not constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act since your income from Mr. Rupp terminated more than twelve months ago. Government Code Section 87103(c).

I caution you, however, that this informal advice does not represent a formal opinion of the Commission. Moreover, the advice is based on the limited facts presented by you and is offered solely in response to those facts.

If you wish to request a review by the full Commission of the denial of your opinion request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have any questions, please contact Kenneth Finney, Director of the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the Commission

[illegible]

Fair Political Practice
Commission
December 4, 1975
Page Two

I have been involved in business endeavors of public
relations and advertising in this county for the past 17 years.
One individual who I have done work for in the past is Mr. Scott
Rupp, who has various business entities within our county. Two
of our board members, one of which was the board member who raised
the question on my being able to vote on the issue, have alleged
that Mr. Scott Rupp would be one of those individuals who would be
investigated by this special task force to be formed. To my own
personal knowledge I am not aware of any illegal land division
activities by Mr. Rupp.

Mr. Rupp, who is the president of Butte Construction, Inc.,
did retain my services for advertising work from a period commencing
February 16, 1972 through April 23, 1973 as set out in the enclosed
ledger sheet which I obtained from Mr. Rupp's records. Also enclosed
are copies of the checks by which he made payment to me for the
services that are as set out in the enclosed purchase orders. Also
enclosed are samples of the work which I did for he and his related
firms. At no time was I ever in an employee category for Mr. Rupp
or any of his firms, but rather acted strictly as an independent
contractor. At the time I did this work for Mr. Rupp, I did business
under my own name, however, I have now established the firm of
Image Impact duly registered as a fictitious name in the records of
the county clerk's office.

It is my understanding that the board member who brought
this matter up, Supervisor Bernard Richter, did have a long discussion
with your Chief Counsel, Mr. Spurlock. I am not aware as to whether
or not he made his allegations relative to my having a conflict of
interest prior to or after his discussion with Mr. Spurlock.
Mr. Richter did state that Mr. Spurlock would not give him an opinion
as to whether or not I had a conflict of interest, but rather that
it would be necessary for me to ask for one directly.

I have no way of knowing at this time whether or not
Mr. Rupp will be doing any business with my advertising firm in the
future or not. I would state that in the event he did request that
I do additional work for him that in all likelihood I would do such
work in that I have had a very good business relationship with
Mr. Rupp and would have no reason not to do business with him.

Therefore, I respectfully request your opinion pursuant to
Government Code §8314 as to whether or not I would have a conflict
of interest in voting on the issue of whether or not to establish a
special task force in our district attorney's office, which task
force's primary duty would be to investigate and prosecute on
violations of the Subdivision Map Act. Because of the fact that this
issue continues to be before our Board of Supervisors, I would certainly
appreciate your very early reply to my request.

Yours truly,

WARD CAMERON

Butte County

LAND OF NATURAL WEALTH AND BEAUTY

COUNTY ADMINISTRATION BUILDING
OROVILLE, CALIFORNIA 95965

BOARD OF SUPERVISORS

JACK McKILLOP JAMES N. LADD JACK J MADIGAN
District 1 District 4 District 3
Chairman

BERNARD D. RICHTER WARD CAMERON
District 2 District 5

NUMEROUS ATTACHMENTS
SEE PAT VAN PELT FOR
COPIES

December 4, 1975

Fair Political Practice
Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

RE: REQUEST FOR OPINION - CONFLICT OF INTEREST

Gentlemen:

This request for your formal opinion is made pursuant
to the provisions of Government Code §8311. I am a member of
the Board of Supervisors of the County of Butte. We presently
have before the board the issue of whether or not to establish
a special task force in the district attorney's office to investigate
illegal lot splits. This unit, if established, would investigate
certain land transactions that have occurred throughout the county,
which investigations would lead to prosecutions, levying of civil
penalties and fines, etc. on people who were engaged in violations
of the State Map Act. During the discussion of this matter before
the Board of Supervisors, one of my fellow board members expressed
concern as to whether or not I would have a conflict of interest in
voting on this issue. This issue has become a very heated and
controversial matter before the Board of Supervisors, and as a
result of this other board member's contentions, I have abstained
so far in discussion of or voting on the issue. I might add
parenthetically like many issues that have come before the Board,
this one seems to come up weekly without being finally resolved,
and I would further anticipate that my situation must be resolved
in that another member, the chairman, has also had the same
allegations made against him by this other board member. In order
for this issue to be resolved finally, assuming that the task force
unit is established, my vote or the other board member who has been
alleged to have the conflict of interest is voting to be necessary
in order to effect a transfer from the reserve for contingency. I
am sure you are aware of the fact that in order to remove funds from
the reserve for contingency that four affirmative votes are required.

SUPERVISORS MEET EVERY TUESDAY AT 9:00 A.M., COUNTY ADMINISTRATION BUILDING, OROVILLE, CALIFORNIA

sections.question: Whether Ward Cameron, a Butte County Supervisor, has a conflict of interest which would preclude him from voting on the issue of establishing a special task force in the district attorney's office to investigate illegal lot splits, given that he had performed advertising and public relations work for Mr. Scott Rupp from February 16, 1972 through April 23, 1973.
sections.conclusion: These facts do not constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act since your income from Mr. Rupp terminated more than twelve months ago. Government Code Section 87103(c). However, this informal advice does not represent a formal opinion of the Commission and is based on the limited facts presented.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a county supervisor have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when voting on a task force to investigate illegal land divisions, given that the supervisor previously performed independent contractor work for a business owner who may be subject to investigation, with that work relationship ending more than 12 months prior?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act because the supervisor's income from Mr. Rupp ended more than 12 months before the vote, satisfying the requirements of Government Code Section 87103(c), though this constitutes informal advice only.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination of a formal opinion request, but with informal advice provided. The document includes both the incoming request letter from Cameron (dated December 4, 1975) and the FPPC response letter (dated December 16, 1975). The response cites Government Code Section 87103(c) regarding the 12-month rule for past income relationships.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Ward Cameron, a Butte County Supervisor, has a conflict of interest which would preclude him from voting on the issue of establishing a special task force in the district attorney's office to investigate illegal lot splits, given that he had performed advertising and public relations work for Mr. Scott Rupp from February 16, 1972 through April 23, 1973.

CONCLUSION: These facts do not constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act since your income from Mr. Rupp terminated more than twelve months ago. Government Code Section 87103(c). However, this informal advice does not represent a formal opinion of the Commission and is based on the limited facts presented.

embedding.summary: FPPC responded to Supervisor Ward Cameron's request for an opinion regarding a potential conflict of interest in voting on establishing a special task force, finding no conflict under the Political Reform Act since his prior business relationship with a potentially affected party had ended over 12 months earlier.
